subtitle_lines: AWS now lets you mount S3 buckets as NFS file systems.
Here's how it works, what it costs, and where it breaks.

slides.heading: Data Friction Is Everywhere
slides.subheading: Different tools expect different storage interfaces. That gap has always required manual bridging.

cards.title: Object vs. File
cards.body: S3 speaks HTTP and object keys. ML training jobs, genomics pipelines, and legacy apps expect POSIX file paths. Bridging the two has always meant copying data between systems.

cards.title: Existing Solutions Fall Short
cards.body: s3fs-fuse and Mountpoint for S3 provide read-mostly access but lack full write semantics. EFS gives you a filesystem but without S3's cost model or durability story.

bottom_takeaway.label: Core tension
bottom_takeaway.body: You want S3's economics and durability with a filesystem's interface. S3 Files is AWS's answer.

cards.title: EFS as the Cache Layer
cards.body: S3 Files uses EFS to provide an NFS v4.2 mount. Files under 128KB are fully cached. Larger files stream directly from S3 at up to 3 GB/s, bypassing the cache. S3 remains the source of truth.

bottom_takeaway.label: Performance
bottom_takeaway.body: 250K read IOPS, ~1ms cached latency, 25K concurrent clients. Data evicts after 30 days of inactivity.

cards.title: Writes sync, not stream
cards.body: Mutations accumulate in EFS with NFS close-to-open consistency. Every ~60 seconds, staged changes sync to S3 as atomic PUTs. If S3 and the filesystem diverge, S3 wins.

bottom_takeaway.label: Watch out
bottom_takeaway.body: All writes route through the EFS cache at $0.06/GB. For write-heavy workloads, this cost adds up quickly. The 32KB minimum per operation also penalizes small-file metadata workloads.

slides.heading: Key Limitations
slides.subheading: These constraints are worth understanding before you adopt S3 Files in production.

items.label: No Atomic Rename
items.description: Directory renames copy-delete every object. Moving 50K files means 50K individual operations.

items.label: 50M Object Cap
items.description: Each mount supports up to 50 million objects. Larger buckets require partitioned mounts.

items.label: 60s Commit Window
items.description: Writes take up to 60 seconds to appear in S3. Workflows needing real-time sync should look elsewhere.

items.label: Locks Ignored by S3 API
items.description: NFS v4.2 locks work across filesystem clients, but S3 API calls bypass them entirely.

items.label: Glacier Excluded
items.description: Objects in Glacier Flexible Retrieval or Deep Archive require an S3 API restore before filesystem access.

items.label: POSIX Key Conflicts
items.description: Some valid S3 keys cannot map to POSIX filenames. Incompatible keys are hidden from the filesystem view.

slides.heading: When Should You Use S3 Files?

decisions.question: Need POSIX access to S3 data?
decisions.answer: Use S3 Files. ML training, genomics, and legacy apps can read S3 directly without copying data.

decisions.question: Write-heavy or rename-intensive?
decisions.answer: Avoid S3 Files. $0.06/GB writes and copy-delete renames get expensive. Use EFS or FSx instead.

decisions.question: Already optimized for the S3 API?
decisions.answer: Keep using it. S3 Files adds a caching layer you don't need.

bottom_takeaway.label: Rule of thumb
bottom_takeaway.body: S3 Files works best for read-heavy POSIX workloads on top of existing S3 data. The further you drift from that pattern, the worse the economics get.

summary_lines: S3 Files mounts buckets as NFS v4.2 file systems via an EFS cache layer.
Reads bypass the cache for large files at 3 GB/s. Writes sync to S3 every 60s.
Part of S3's evolution: Tables, Vectors, and now Files.
Best for read-heavy POSIX workloads. Watch the write costs.
